name: Min
description: Min was an ancient Egyptian god whose cult originated in the Predynastic period (4th millennium BCE). He was represented in many different forms, but is most often depicted as a male figure with an erect penis, a flail, and a crown of feathers. Min was primarily a god of male virility, fertility, and reproduction. He was also associated with the Eastern Desert, mining, and trade routes leading into that region. As a fertility god, he was believed to ensure the success of crops and the continuation of life. Festivals were held in his honor, particularly during the harvest season, where offerings were made to ensure the bounty of the land. The lettuce, believed to be an aphrodisiac, was often associated with Min. He was also seen as a protector of travelers and miners, guiding them through the harsh desert landscapes. In later periods, Min became associated with Horus, sometimes being referred to as Min-Horus. His worship was widespread throughout Egypt, with important cult centers located at Coptos and Akhmim. He embodies the power and vitality of the natural world, specifically the generative force that sustains life and ensures prosperity.
subtitle: God of Fertility, Virility, and Reproduction

keyMyths.title: Min's Harvest Festival
keyMyths.description: During Min's harvest festival, the pharaoh would symbolically cut the first sheaf of grain to honor the god and ensure a bountiful harvest. This ritual represented the god's role in agricultural fertility and the prosperity of the kingdom.

keyMyths.title: Min and the Lettuce
keyMyths.description: Lettuce was considered sacred to Min due to its milky sap, which was associated with semen. Offerings of lettuce were made to Min to enhance his virility and ensure successful reproduction.

keyMyths.title: Min's Desert Associations
keyMyths.description: Min's connection to the Eastern Desert stems from his role as a protector of travelers and miners. He was believed to guide them through the dangerous landscapes and ensure their safe passage.

sources.author: Harpur, Yvonne
